metadata.title: Northwind AI - Lead AI Engineer - R2 Technical + System Design - Meera Krishnan

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Hi Meera, thanks for making the time. I'm Priya, Principal AI Engineer, and Marcus here is our Staff Engineer for system design. This round is about 60 minutes of technical depth, a design exercise, and some discussion on leading delivery. Ready when you are.

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: Thanks Priya, happy to be here. I'm a Senior AI Engineer at BrightPath, about four years in, mostly building RAG systems and LLM-powered search and support tools.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Great. Walk me through the most complex multi-agent system you've built and how you orchestrated it.

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: The main one is a customer-support assistant. It's a RAG pipeline - retrieval over our docs in a vector store, then an LLM that can call a few tools like ticket lookup and order status. It handles a good chunk of tier-1 tickets, so I'd call it our multi-agent support system.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Bell
transcript.text: Just to be precise - was that several agents coordinating, or a single agent calling tools? Have you orchestrated multiple agents that hand off to each other?

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: Fair - in production it's really one agent with tool calls. I prototyped a planner-and-executor split in a side project, but it never went to production. So multi-agent orchestration at scale is honestly newer territory for me.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: How did you evaluate quality on the support assistant?

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: That part I'm solid on. I built an offline eval set of a few hundred real queries with expected answers, ran LLM-as-judge for answer relevance and faithfulness, and tracked those metrics on every prompt change before shipping. It caught several regressions.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Bell
transcript.text: In production, how did you handle governance, auditability, and observability?

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: We logged requests and responses to a dashboard and had some latency and error metrics. We didn't really have formal compliance requirements, so there wasn't a structured audit trail or policy layer - I haven't worked much with things like NIST or model-risk frameworks.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Bell
transcript.text: Let's design. Sketch an agentic platform that automates first-round technical interview screening, end to end.

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: Okay - I'd ingest the interview transcript and the candidate resume, chunk and embed them, then run a RAG step to pull the relevant parts against the job description. Then an LLM scores the candidate on a rubric and outputs a recommendation. I'd store results in a database and show them in a dashboard for the recruiter to review.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Bell
transcript.text: Where do human approvals and the downstream actions - emails, ATS updates, scheduling - fit? And how is the lifecycle managed?

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: I'd probably have the recruiter click approve in the dashboard before anything is sent, and then call the ATS and email APIs. I haven't designed that lifecycle in a lot of detail before - I'd want to think more about how the pieces coordinate.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Bell
transcript.text: What about failure modes - the model hallucinates or returns malformed output?

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: I'd add retries, and maybe a validation step to check the output format. If it's clearly wrong we'd fall back to a default or flag it. I'll admit I haven't formalized confidence thresholds or an escalation flow in production.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Scale and cost for hundreds of concurrent sessions?

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: I'd add more workers behind a queue and cache the embeddings so we're not recomputing them. Beyond that - things like model tiering or backpressure - I know the terms but haven't had to tune a system at that scale myself.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Tell me about leading a team or making a hard cross-team call.

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: I led a two-engineer squad on the support-assistant feature - I planned the work and reviewed their PRs, and coordinated with one other team on the API. I haven't owned cross-organization contracts or led a larger group yet, but stepping up to that kind of leadership is exactly why I'm interested in a lead role.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Bell
transcript.text: A weakness or growth area you're working on?

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: Honestly, going deeper on true multi-agent orchestration and on production governance and observability. My depth is in RAG and evaluation; the agentic-platform and compliance side is where I most want to grow.

transcript.speaker: Meera Krishnan
transcript.text: One question from me - for someone stepping up into this lead role, what kind of ramp-up or mentorship would there be on the multi-agent and platform side?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Good question, and a fair one to ask. We'll take that into account. Thanks Meera - appreciate the candor, and we'll follow up on next steps.

candidate.name: Meera Krishnan
highlights: Built and shipped a production RAG-based customer-support assistant handling tier-1 tickets
Owns an offline LLM evaluation pipeline (LLM-as-judge) gating prompt changes

role.title: Lead AI Engineer
nice_to_have_skills: FinServ compliance (NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / SR 11-7)
jd_highlights: Own architecture and delivery of Northwind's multi-agent operations platform
Lead and grow a team of AI engineers
Set the standards for evaluation, governance, and observability

tracker.notes: Cleared recruiter screen and R1. Strong RAG/evaluation background; applying up-level from Senior to Lead.
